paragraphs.body: In the winter when I was eight years old, my family moved to a skyscraper of an apartment complex, and we were living on the 25th floor. As for the reason why we lived so high, my father told me, "You have to stand high to see far." I love this house a lot, not only because of the unique design of all floor-to-ceiling windows, but all these beautiful scenes I was able to see every day. At night, I could always see cars speeding on the brightly lit road thought the transparent window that’s in the kitchen. On the roadside were shining high-rise buildings which you can see in a fantasy novel, and fireworks can be seen blooming in the sky from time to time. The arrival of spring was occasionally accompanied by heavy fog. On foggy days, I felt like I’m living in a house that’s on clouds. The world looks like doomsday. The big trees were looming in the fog, and the sky was filled with gray mist.

annotations.context_left: all these beautiful scenes I was able to see every day. At night, I could always see cars

paragraphs.body: In the summer, because there was no wall, the kitchen was like an oven. But leaning against the window, I could see green vines climbing around the fence and purple flowers on the rooftop of an apartment that was shorter than my house. At that time, I liked to watch the owner doing busy work on the rooftop in the early morning. He was an old man who always took care of the plants alone with a watering can. Looking at him, I always imagined that I would have my own rooftop for planting when I grew up. Although I love plants, there were no plants in my room. The only thing that could be considered a plant was the flowers I drew. Whenever I was so into watching that old man cleaning his garden, I would smell the aroma of food emanating from the kitchen pot. It was the smell of stewed soup - beef, corn and carrots mixed to emit a sweet smell. At this time, my mother would ask me to serve the bowls and chopsticks because it’s time to eat. I didn't hesitate because I was used to dividing the tableware for the whole family before every meal. If everyone hadn't come to the table after I’m done, I would scoop a spoonful of rice and a bowl of soup for everyone. Although I would complain in my heart that why am I the only one doing things every time, I knew that it was useless. My mother often said, “You are the eldest kid in our family, and this is your responsibility to set a good model for your younger brothers and sisters.” And my father once said, "There is no absolute fairness in the world, but the eldest child should know how to yield and take care of the younger ones, because it is our traditional virtue. This is your responsibility." I don't understand and I don't agree. Should I say this society is toxic? Not really, because this is a virtue. But if I say this society is not toxic, then why do it force us to do things that are unfair? Everyone says, "Good children get candy", but for society, good children are expected to share candy with their younger siblings and other kids, which basically equals to "Good children actually don't get candy". So, what is our responsibility? Is it the mermaid in the fairy tale turning into foam? Or do we have to fight for others in the front?

So, what is our responsibility? Is it the mermaid in the fairy tale turning into foam? Or do we have to fight for others in the front?

paragraphs.body: Days passed, and I grew up gradually. During this period, I had arguments with my mother and moved several times. Since I was a child, I have been fed up with the unfairness I have received. Even though I am the oldest child in the family, I am not the head of power among my brothers and sisters. My younger brother was. He had the best computer in our family and the largest room. His responsibility was to play games in the room after school every day. While I needed to put the washed clothes in a big basket with my sister in the hot summer sun, take them outside, and hang them on clothes hooks. Then carry the dry clothes from the backyard to the second floor in the afternoon. Then put away the clothes hooks, sort the clothes, and fold them. Occasionally, I need to send the clothes where they belong. Whenever I ask why boys don’t have to do these things, the answer I get was always, "You are a girl, you will have to do it when you grow up, this is your responsibility."

annotations.comment: the way you repeat this phrase is brilliant.

paragraphs.body: Every time before dinner, my mother would ask me to go downstairs to help her cook. Although it was not difficult, it was boring to do. Especially at 5 pm, the sky was gloomy, and everyone was on the second floor. Although I am not very afraid of ghosts, this atmosphere makes me feel lonely. I would need to find the pot of rice in the lifeless kitchen, then use a transparent white plastic bowl that had some scratches to scoop up the rice. Then pour it into the iron pot, rinse it with water, and slowly rotate the rice with my hands to make sure there was no dirt in the rice. Then repeat it twice, but make sure not to wash it "too clean" because then it will not have the rice smell. Finally, add oil and press start. The timing song that I have heard countless times sounded again. And every time I complain about why do I have to cook, why not my younger brothers and sisters? The answer I get was always, "You are the eldest, it is your responsibility to help this family do what you can."

My room was the only room in our house with wooden floors besides the kitchen and laundry room. The side effect of having such a room was that I was the only one in the family who needed to mop the floor by hand every week, while my younger brothers and sisters could lie on the bed and be on their phones every week, while just waiting for the robot vacuum cleaner to take care of everything. I often complain that the mop was difficult to use. Compared to being a mop, I think it is more suitable for Halloween custom wigs. It looks like a piece of cloth cut into strips, folded in half, and then stable it in the middle. When I mop the floor, I can always hear the creaking sound of the plastic that used to stabilize the mop rubbing against the ground. Not only that, I also needed to sweep the floor every week, otherwise the corners would become a kingdom of dust and spider silk. If you were unlucky, you could also see rat tails coming out of the pipes under the heater. Although I like fairy tales a lot, living in Cinderella's room was not fun at all. And every time I complain, the answer I get is always, "This is your room, you are responsible for keeping it clean."

Whenever I finish cleaning, I will open the window to try to fill the room with fresh air. With the wind chimes tinkling above my head, I often think, why are these my responsibilities? I have no right to choose the order of my birth. I have no right to choose whether I want to be a boy or a girl. I have no right to choose whether my room can have carpets. But all these things that do not belong to me, this society requires me to be responsible for them. Just like leaves, they have no right to choose their color, and no right to choose their type. Some leaves can become handicrafts, while others are discarded in the trash and slowly rot.

annotations.comment: should you return to the man and his gardens here? You could talk about the leaves and growing things naturally
annotations.context_left: fresh air. With the wind chimes tinkling above my head, I often think, why are these

paragraphs.body: When I was 17, I liked to look out the window of my room because I could see colorful leaves like a fairytale town. At night, I could see the gradient night sky and occasionally see stars shining like diamonds. There was a small old tree in my room. I often forget to water it on weekdays, but it always stays alive. I am still not sure what kind of tree it is, but each branch of it has five oval-shaped leaves, which together look like a duck's claw. As the seasons change, the leaves of this tree will also become dry. Then they will fall to the ground, and then I will clean them up. Just like I have to clean up the fallen leaves every autumn.

annotations.text: Just like I have to clean up the fallen leaves every autum

paragraphs.body: As the year approached October, the fall was coming. All the leaves lose their original green and put on their colorful clothes. Since the afternoon, my sister, my mother, and I have been carrying leaf-gathering tools to clean up the front yard. Like in previous years, I used a tool that was two heads taller than me to scrape the leaves on the grass that were mixing with the color of green and wheat. I usually think that we were fixing the “hair” of the earth to make it a more interesting task. Using the tool, we piled up vibrant leaves into a mountain. Behind me was the rustling sound of the leaves colliding. A fresh smell of rain, grass, and fragrance of flowers hit my nose, and the sounds of tires hum as the car runs through the road. Sometimes, we can hear mirrors whistling in the wind, and the autumn wind sweeps like waves. The leaves start dancing in the air. With the melody of the wind, the leaves spin and dance in the air like graceful dancers. When the wind was finally enough of playing, all the leaves fell gently in front of me and scattered on the grass, forming a unique autumn painting.

annotations.text: Sometimes, we can hear mirrors whistling in the wind, and the autumn wind sweeps like waves.

paragraphs.body: Then, I will have to do everything all over again. It was so annoying. But my mom had told me--this is my responsibility to clean the leaves even though they do not belong to me. Holding a huge tool made me tired. My hand started to shake after an hour. I could feel the heat coming from my body as I breathe rhythmically. But it did not stop me because I knew times were passing away. Next year, I will be leaving the house. Every time I found a beautiful leave, I would pick it up in the palm of my hand, look at it, feel the textures, and then just throw it back into the leaf pile. Even it’s a maple leaf that has the color of sunset. I could feel the rough touch but a hint of coldness. The unevenness of the edge reminds me of the gear and wood pruner. These dry leaves were witnesses of time.

At the age of 18, I no longer need to dry clothes, cook every night, use that difficult mop, or clean up fallen leaves. But when I’m gone, who will be the one replacing me? Who will clean up the fallen leaves for me and do these responsibilities that originally belonged to me? To me, leaves are not a plant, not a tree organ. The leaves were once green on the branches, but now they are golden. Leaves are witnesses of time. Their growth and withering reflect the changes in seasons and life. Leaves are a dry river, washing away people's past regrets. Leaves are the proof of injustice and repeatedly questioning cannot bring justice. Leaves are the recording of memories and vicissitudes. Leaves are those people who go against the wind and try to change but are blown down by the wind. Leaves are a transparent wall, trapping those who are also expected by society and cannot speak out. Leaves are wings that cannot fly. Leaves are the responsibility of the passing of time. Leaves are a pair of hands, stealing the youth of children and leaving behind persistence and complaints.

annotations.text: But when I’m gone, who will be the one
replacing me? Who will clean up the fallen leaves for me and do these responsibilities that originally belonged to me?
